title: Professions (Sci-Fi)
description: Spacefaring careers — what people in a space-faring setting do for work, usable as instant NPC context.

entries.result: Cargo hauler — independent operator running freight between stations on a small personal ship.
Jump navigator — specialist who plots faster-than-light routes and manages the calculations that can't be trusted to automation.
Atmospheric processor technician — maintains the systems that make worlds breathable, mostly alone, mostly on sites no one wants to visit.
Station medic — covers everything from zero-g injuries to whatever comes in off the docks, no questions asked.
Xenobiologist — studies life on newly catalogued worlds, usually underfunded and usually right about things that get ignored.
Ship salvager — strips derelict vessels for parts, pays a percentage to the claim holder, keeps whatever the manifest missed.
Colony surveyor — assesses new sites for habitability and sends reports that determine whether a location is developed or abandoned.
Comm relay technician — maintains the communication infrastructure between systems, spending months at isolated relay stations.
Weapons inspector — certifies that ships and installations are within treaty-mandated arms limits, with appropriate latitude for interpretation.
Deep space miner — works extraction rigs on asteroid fields and moons, long rotations, high pay, significant risk.
Diplomatic courier — carries communications and small diplomatic payloads between jurisdictions that don't fully trust electronic transmission.
Terraforming engineer — manages the long-cycle processes that convert barren worlds into habitable ones, on timelines that exceed any single career.
Station security — enforces rules on orbital platforms where jurisdictional authority is ambiguous and backup is always thirty minutes away.
Exogeologist — studies the geology of other worlds, finding resources, identifying hazards, and cataloguing things that don't fit known models.
Bounty retrieval agent — tracks and recovers persons or property across jurisdictional boundaries under a private warrant.
Ship mechanic — keeps vessels operational by any means available, with a preference for solutions that require whatever parts are currently on hand.
Customs inspector — processes passengers and cargo at transit points, paid modestly, offered supplements that exceed their salary.
Agricultural tech — runs food production systems on colony worlds and stations, where the margin between enough and not enough is always visible.
Energy systems engineer — maintains the reactors, solar arrays, and power distribution networks that keep everything running.
Transmission analyst — monitors deep space signals for content of interest, filing reports to organizations that may or may not act on them.
Environmental hazard technician — remediates contaminated sites on worlds and stations, equipped for scenarios that most people won't enter.
First contact protocol officer — trained for encounters with previously unknown intelligences; has never been deployed but maintains readiness.
Fleet logistics coordinator — manages supply chains across multiple systems, ensuring that what is needed arrives where it's needed before it's critical.
Refugee intake specialist — processes displaced populations from conflict zones, disaster sites, and failed colonies.
Ship's cook — maintains nutritional standards and morale on long voyages through a combination of competence and stubbornness.
Orbital traffic controller — manages the movement of vessels in and out of a busy system, under conditions of chronic understaffing.
Genetic archivist — catalogs and preserves genetic material from species and ecosystems before they are altered or lost.
Smuggler — operates outside legal shipping channels, specializing in whatever the current regulatory environment makes most profitable.
Translator — handles communication between species and cultures that haven't achieved mutual intelligibility through technology alone.
Security consultant — advises stations and ships on vulnerability, threat assessment, and incident response, based on methods that don't appear in official training.
Cybernetic maintenance tech — services implants and prosthetics, working on both authorized hardware and modifications that aren't in any manufacturer's catalog.
Astrophysicist — researches stellar phenomena, usually from a university posting, occasionally dragged along on missions where the phenomena are more immediate.
Black market broker — connects buyers and sellers of regulated materials, acting as a neutral party who maintains plausible deniability.
Ship's pilot — flies vessels for hire, maintaining strict professional neutrality about cargo and destination.
Corporate spy — gathers competitive intelligence for one faction while presenting as an employee of another.
Derelict explorer — legally or otherwise, enters abandoned ships and stations to recover assets before they're claimed or destroyed.
Emergency response coordinator — manages rescue and relief operations across multiple systems, always operating with fewer resources than needed.
Fuel depot operator — runs the stations that keep ships moving, at intersections of travel that give them access to an enormous amount of information.
Xenolinguist — works on deciphering non-human communication systems, making progress measured in years.
Shipwright — designs and builds vessels, with strong opinions about what other shipbuilders are getting wrong.
Information broker — buys and sells data, intelligence, and secrets, operating from a node in a network no one has successfully mapped.
Medical researcher — studies conditions unique to space travel and extended habitation in hostile environments.
Jump gate technician — maintains the infrastructure that makes travel between systems viable, at installations that are critical and perpetually underfunded.
Colony administrator — manages the day-to-day operations of a developing settlement, usually with authority that exceeds their actual power.
Ship's counselor — provides psychological support on long voyages, where isolation, confinement, and stress are occupational constants.
Asteroid claim assessor — determines the validity of mining claims and the value of finds, with significant leverage over who gets rich.
Void diver — works outside ships and stations in vacuum, doing repairs and extractions that can't be done from inside.
Combat medic — provides field medicine in conflict zones, under conditions that official medical doctrine doesn't cover.
Historian — documents the events and cultures of the expansion era, working against the tendency of spacefaring civilizations to let records lapse.
Propulsion engineer — develops and maintains the drive systems that determine what travel is possible.
Cartographer — maps newly explored regions, a job that sounds academic and is frequently dangerous.
Quarantine officer — manages isolation protocols after contact with unknown biological material, where the margin for error is zero.
Ship's gunner — operates weapons systems, maintains them, and waits for situations where they are necessary.
Cryogenics technician — manages long-haul passenger hibernation, responsible for the lives of people who are completely dependent and completely unaware.
Debris removal specialist — clears hazardous materials from orbital lanes, a job in perpetual demand in any busy system.
Pirate — takes what they can from ships that can't or won't fight back, operating in the gaps between patrol routes.
Artificial intelligence ethicist — advises on the design, deployment, and limitation of autonomous systems, with views that are inconvenient for manufacturers.
Colony teacher — educates the children of settlers, covering a range of subjects no single person is fully qualified to teach.
Sublight courier — runs packages and messages on shorter routes where the economics don't support faster travel.
Merchant — buys, sells, and trades across systems, with a ship that is equally cargo hold and home.
Sensor technician — interprets the data produced by scanning equipment, bridging the gap between what the machines detect and what it means.
Interpreter for synthetic intelligences — facilitates communication between AI systems and human operators who don't share communication protocols.
Investigative journalist — covers the stories that official sources won't, usually from a position of financial and personal precarity.
Ship's accountant — manages the financial operations of a vessel, where the difference between profit and insolvency is always visible.
Planetary defense coordinator — manages the systems that protect settled worlds from asteroid strikes, solar events, and other hazards.
Synthetic biologist — engineers biological systems for specific functions, working at the intersection of science and regulation.
Wreck diver — recovers passengers and cargo from ships lost in transit, as a contracted service for insurers and families.
Faction liaison — maintains communication and relationships between political entities that need to talk without appearing to.
Surveyor drone operator — manages remote exploration equipment across distances that make real-time control impossible.
Interstellar lawyer — practices in the areas where multiple legal systems overlap, where the gaps are as important as the statutes.
Radiation safety officer — monitors and manages exposure across environments where shielding is imperfect or compromised.
Fleet admiral — commands multiple vessels in coordinated operations, from a flagship that is itself a small city.
Waste reclamation engineer — manages the systems that convert waste into reusable resources, on ships and stations where nothing can be discarded.
Retired marine — spent years in close-quarters combat in zero gravity environments, and has since found that most civilian work is easier.
Xenoanthropologist — studies the social structures and cultures of non-human intelligences, usually at a respectful distance.
Ship's librarian — maintains the data archives that ships carry as a matter of survival, on voyages where external connection cannot be guaranteed.
Corporate compliance officer — ensures that company operations remain within the letter of various jurisdictional regulations, with flexibility on the spirit.
Emergency shelter coordinator — manages the logistics of housing large populations displaced by disaster or conflict.
System administrator for station AI — maintains and occasionally argues with the distributed intelligence that runs a station's core functions.
Hazardous materials pilot — flies transports carrying cargo that no automated system is trusted to move.
Arms dealer — supplies weapons to buyers who have reasons for not going through official channels.
Jump drive researcher — studies faster-than-light mechanics with the aim of improving, replacing, or circumventing current technology.
Colony physician — provides comprehensive healthcare to a settlement that can't afford specialization.
Prisoner transport officer — moves detained individuals between facilities across jurisdictions that don't always cooperate.
Water reclamation technician — manages the systems that make water available on ships and stations, where failure is not recoverable in the short term.
Mercenary — provides military capability for hire, with a specialty that has developed from repeated application.
Habitat designer — engineers the living and working spaces that humans occupy in hostile environments, where ergonomics is a survival concern.
Long-haul passenger attendant — manages the needs and wellbeing of travelers on voyages measured in months, with complete responsibility and minimal support.
Signal intelligence analyst — intercepts and interprets communications, operating in a legal grey area that is convenient for everyone except the subject.
Xenoarchaeologist — excavates and analyzes the remains of civilizations that predate human expansion, on worlds that sometimes don't want them there.
Clone rights advocate — argues on behalf of artificial humans in legal systems that are still deciding what artificial humans are.
Navigation AI supervisor — monitors the decisions made by automated navigation systems and overrides them when the system is technically right but practically wrong.
Military contractor — provides services to armed forces without being formally part of them, with corresponding flexibility in what they'll do and how.
Biodome manager — maintains the self-contained ecological systems that provide food and atmosphere on long-distance stations and ships.
Quantum communication technician — operates the systems that allow instantaneous communication across vast distances, when and where they work.
Fixer — solves problems for clients across a range of difficulty, using a network built over a career of being useful to people who need things done quietly.
Decommissioning specialist — safely retires ships, stations, and reactors at end of operational life, ensuring that what they leave behind doesn't cause problems.
Exopsychologist — studies the psychological effects of space travel, alien contact, and extended isolation on the human mind.
Station administrator — manages the political, operational, and social complexities of a large orbital installation that is simultaneously a city, a port, and a government.
Whatever they need to be. This person has held seventeen jobs across twelve systems. Their qualifications are broad, their references are complicated, and their current role is temporary.
